summary: This post presents the distilled key points from the book 'NoSQL Distilled' by Pramod Sadalage and Martin Fowler. It covers why NoSQL emerged as an alternative to relational databases, particularly for handling large data volumes on clusters. The content explains different data models (aggregate-oriented, graph), distribution patterns (sharding, replication), consistency tradeoffs, and practical concerns like schema migrations. The overarching message emphasizes polyglot persistence—using the right database technology for each specific need rather than one-size-fits-all. The authors caution that most applications should stick with relational databases until NoSQL matures further.
key_points: NoSQL emerged primarily from the need to handle large data volumes across clusters, which relational databases weren't designed for efficiently
Aggregate-oriented databases (key-value, document, column-family) organize data around units of interaction, making cluster distribution easier but cross-aggregate relationships harder

Consistency in distributed systems involves tradeoffs: you can choose between availability and consistency during network partitions (CAP theorem), and between consistency and latency in normal operations
Polyglot persistence—using different storage technologies for different needs—is the most important outcome of the NoSQL movement
Most applications should stick with relational technology until NoSQL ecosystems mature, and any NoSQL adoption should be tested against actual productivity and performance expectations
money_quotes: The vital factor for a change in data storage was the need to support large volumes of data by running on clusters. Relational databases are not designed to run efficiently on clusters.
NoSQL is an accidental neologism. There is no prescriptive definition—all you can make is an observation of common characteristics.
The most important result of the rise of NoSQL is Polyglot Persistence.
Schemaless databases allow you to freely add fields to records, but there is usually an implicit schema expected by users of the data.
To get good consistency, you need to involve many nodes in data operations, but this increases latency. So you often have to trade off consistency versus latency.
Adding more data storage technologies increases complexity in programming and operations, so the advantages of a good data storage fit need to be weighed against this complexity.
Most applications, particularly nonstrategic ones, should stick with relational technology—at least until the NoSQL ecosystem becomes more mature.
key_insight: NoSQL's real contribution isn't replacing relational databases but enabling polyglot persistence—matching storage technology to specific data needs while acknowledging the complexity tradeoffs involved.
